_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning that electricity can be generated as AC using magnets and turbines (powered by wind, water, steam or nuclear energy) or as DC using solar cells or batteries.

_source.w: We are learning that electricity can be generated as AC using magnets and turbines (powered by wind, water, steam or nuclear energy) or as DC using solar cells or batteries.
y: Understanding how electricity is generated connects the abstract concepts of electromagnetism to the practical infrastructure that powers our homes, schools and industries.

Comparing different generation methods — including their efficiency, environmental impact and reliability — develops our capacity to evaluate energy policy decisions that affect our communities and the planet.
As Australia transitions toward renewable energy, understanding the science behind different generation technologies helps us participate in informed conversations about our energy future.
eg: Modelling electromagnetic induction by moving a magnet through a coil of wire and detecting the induced current, demonstrating the fundamental principle behind all generators.

Comparing how a coal-fired power station, wind turbine, hydroelectric dam and nuclear reactor all ultimately spin a turbine to generate AC electricity, but differ in their energy source and environmental impact.
Investigating how a solar cell converts light energy directly into DC electricity using the photovoltaic effect, without any moving parts.
Explaining the difference between AC (alternating current, used for mains power and long-distance transmission) and DC (direct current, used in batteries and electronic devices).

Analysing the advantages and limitations of different renewable energy sources for Australia — solar (abundant, intermittent), wind (low emissions, variable), hydro (reliable, geographically limited).
Discussing how battery storage technology and smart grid systems are addressing the challenge of integrating intermittent renewable energy sources into a reliable electricity supply.
